Then in magnitude pruning, we would just set b to zero.
And then the model would just become a times x because the idea is that this.
This parameter has a lot more effect on the output and therefore we don't need to consider the other one.

The problem is that you can make a model sparse you can take the vectors and make them sparse.
But if you're hardware does not take advantage of that sparsity, then you're not getting any gains and performance.

And so. So basically right now this is not very effective in the current hardware, although I hope this will change in the future.
So therefore like a more immediately useful idea is called structured pruning.
And the idea here is that instead of just picking parameters across with willy nilly across the whole model, you remove entire components or entire layers.
And therefore you're ruining the model in a way that is structured and really going to make a difference on your overall runtime.
So Graham and one of his PhD students a few years ago did some really cool work on this where they showed that.

If you're training a transformer model like Bert, you usually have many heads of attention like you guys also experienced this for the llama homework where you had, I think it was eight heads of attention there.
But in practice, most of these heads of attention can be removed without really any.
Negative impact on the performance of your model.
And so here they show that for a empty model, you can remove half of the attention heads entirely and get a negligible impact on your performance.
This is different than what we showed here where we were removing parameters from anywhere in the model we saw fit.
Here we're removing entire heads of attention, even some of those heads might have large magnitude weights, some of them might have small magnitude weights, but.
We can just remove the entire attention head and this has a immediate impact on the performance of your model.
And generalizing this recent work has proposed controlling even other kinds of components of your model.

In this paper from from two years ago, they proposed masking having two levels of masks on your model. The first is like what they call a course mask, which is turning off large components like full attention heads are full forward layers where you replace them with identity matrix.
And these are like really big things to turn off. And then you could also have fine masks like sorry, I meant entire self-pension layers, not attention heads.
And then the fine mask to control like individual heads or removing individual dimensions.
Changing your hidden state to be from like 512 dimensions to 200 dimensions.
And so the idea here is they give two different levels of granularity at which you can turn off different components.
And then these masks learn using some kind of held out validation data to learn what can we turn off without like totally destroying the performance of this model.
And in this paper, they showed that you can really get pretty far with this idea.
And the last thing I'll say about pruning here is that methods like this, you're actually learning a kind of control over your your model. So you're learning these this set of of masks.

So one of this is a recent paper from Graham and one of his PhD students where they instead ask can we do pruning without having to like compute gradients at all.
And basically, if we just have enough memory to run the model on our computer, can we then use that same computer to prune the model without having to do any to use add a more to compute gradients.
And so I think the idea here is really clever. They basically randomly masked out all the different modules in the network.
They create like 100 or 1000 variants of this model with different masks turned off. Then they measure the performance of those like perturb models.
And then they learn a regression of like how much does each module affect the performance of the full system.
And then you can then use these regression weights of this train of this learned regressor to figure out which modules you can actually turn off without impacting the performance too much.
